raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a robotic head, labeled 'Furhat Robotics,' featuring a human-like face with blue eyes and a furry hat. It speaks with a synthesized voice, saying "Keep guessing," as two men, one with glasses and a dark sweater and the other in a blue shirt, sit across from it. The man in the blue shirt asks, "Is it a boat? Is it a boat?" while engaging in conversation around a large interactive screen. A female narrator explains that Furhat will look and sound more human but won't be identical, as studies show a preference for a clear distinction between humans and robots to avoid it being "too creepy." The scene transitions to a concert stage where the band ABBA performs under a massive illuminated 'ABBA' sign, with members in sparkling outfits and dynamic lighting, as their iconic song "Gimme! Gimme! Gimme! (A Man After Midnight)" plays with full vocals. The narrator continues, noting that the boundaries are blurred in media, and after 40 years, ABBA is back on stage not in real life, but as avatars, with only their voices still real. The video then cuts to a black-and-white split screen showing a woman with a motion capture suit on the left and a digital avatar on the right, illustrating facial mapping technology, followed by a recording studio scene where a woman with white hair sings into a vintage microphone while a man with glasses and a striped shirt observes. The final segment features a man with long wavy hair, wearing a black polo shirt, speaking expressively in French on a sunlit balcony overlooking a cityscape, gesturing with his hands as he talks. A male voiceover translates his speech, where he states, "We are at the start of a huge technological shift," predicting that real-life voice recordings will become superfluous in a few years.

The segment opens with a man with long, wavy hair and a black polo shirt, standing on a balcony with a cityscape background, speaking directly to the camera about creating new synthetic voices using scientific principles, followed by a French voice-over reiterating the concept. The scene then transitions to a dark, cinematic clip from *2001: A Space Odyssey*, where an astronaut in a space suit inside a spacecraft is heard telling HAL, 'I don't know what you're talking about, Hal.' A close-up of HAL 9000's iconic red, glowing eye appears as its calm, synthesized voice responds, 'I know that you and Frank were planning to disconnect me,' continuing, 'And I'm afraid that's something I cannot allow to happen,' while the spacecraft's control room with the astronaut and HAL's interface is shown. This is followed by a futuristic, animated sequence of a server room with cascading digital code and glowing blue lights, as a female narrator explains that 'Artificial intelligence making independent decisions was still a pipe dream in director Stanley Kubrick's day,' but 'Today, we live with it. And maybe that's a good thing, because AI doesn't make emotional decisions or succumb to alluring voices.' Prof. Jon Freeman, identified as 'Director of Social Cognitive & Neural Science Lab,' is then shown speaking expressively, as the narrator concludes, 'It's neutral and impartial, and everything we're not, right?' The final shot is a clip from the TV show *Lie To Me*, depicting a man in a dark sweater in an interrogation-like setting, while a male voice (likely Prof. Jon Freeman) explains that if an AI is programmed with media depictions linking 'African American' with 'hostility and crime,' it will 'pick up on that and act accordingly.'

In a dimly lit, modern conference room, a man in a black sweater with white shoulder panels gestures towards a large screen displaying a black-and-white image of John F. Kennedy labeled 'Inspiring,' as police officers in blue uniforms observe attentively. A female narrator with a British accent explains that AI is more like us than we realize, showing how some faces are automatically linked to stereotypes. The scene shifts to a woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, holding a clipboard, standing beside a man seated at a desk with multiple monitors. These monitors display faces and numerical scores, including 'Avg. Assoc. Time African American: 3.4' and 'Caucasian: 1.3,' indicating a racial bias test. During this, a female voice with an American accent states, 'we're testing for racial bias,' followed by a male voice with an American accent declaring, 'there are racists,' and confirming, '80% of people who take this test are biased.' The video then cuts to Prof. Jon Freeman, a man in a dark blue button-up shirt, speaking expressively in a softly lit room. The female narrator returns, explaining that subconscious bias directly impacts our perceptions and association time. Prof. Freeman then elaborates, describing how these biases leave 'a lot of collateral damage in the brain,' not just as a 'stereotype living in sort of a filing cabinet,' but changing 'approach and avoidance tendencies, behavioral tendencies, motor tendencies, visual tendencies, auditory tendencies.' The final sequence shows Prof. Freeman in a medical imaging lab, assisting a person on an MRI scanner bed, then operating the scanner from a control room, monitoring brain scans displayed on a computer screen. The female narrator explains that 'John Freeman is looking at exactly what happens using fMRI,' showing a test group several different faces, and how the part of the brain responsible for facial recognition, along with other areas processing social and emotional information, are also activated, concluding with a shot of a 3D brain model with highlighted regions.

The minute opens with an upbeat, slightly mysterious background music as a female narrator introduces concepts of “memorized bias and personality traits,” and how “our brains make fast predictions” by registering “what we perceive to be most probable.” Visually, a man in a dark blue button-up shirt is seen in a dimly lit control room, intently analyzing detailed MRI brain scans on multiple monitors, which then transition to a close-up of the scans with grid overlays and timestamps. A patient is briefly shown lying inside a large white MRI scanner, their feet visible. The man in the blue shirt then speaks expressively with animated hand gestures, illuminated only by the screen's glow, explaining how the brain's predictive mechanisms are “adaptive,” using the example of expecting “chairs and tables and a waiter” in a restaurant to avoid wasting “metabolic resources” and “the brain's time” on “processing every single object.” He elaborates that the brain will “generate a bunch of hypotheses” and “use expectations to fill in the gaps” it's “too lazy to figure out itself,” and “doesn't want to waste its resources.” As the music returns, the scene shifts to a brightly lit hospital corridor where a man in a black shirt and face mask walks alongside a woman in a light gray hijab and blue striped shirt, both masked. The female narrator then questions, “So are we merely at the mercy of these mechanisms?” before stating that “John Freeman refuses to accept this theory” and “is carrying out research to find out how...” The video concludes with a computer screen displaying a digital face labeled 'Person #12' with the text 'Shaded at a young person of ripped clothes,' followed by a close-up of hands typing on a black keyboard, one hand adorned with a red wristband and a delicate gold bracelet.

The segment opens with a woman in a light gray hijab and blue and white striped shirt, wearing a light blue surgical mask, seated in a brightly lit clinical or research corridor, accompanied by a man in a black shirt and mask standing behind her. A female narrator's voice explains, "We learn these stereotypes and whether we can unlearn them." The scene transitions to a digital screen displaying a bald, neutral digital face labeled 'Person #10' with the text 'Donated clothes to a shelter for homeless youth,' as the narrator adds, "He shows the test group a range of faces linked to specific character traits." The video then cuts to Prof. Jon Freeman, a man with short dark hair in a dark blue button-up shirt, seated in a softly lit room, speaking expressively with animated hand gestures. His clear, explanatory voice takes over, stating, "So given all that, we wanted to explore our capacity to rapidly acquire completely novel facial stereotypes out of thin air." A close-up of Prof. Freeman shows a digital face on a screen beside him, displaying adjustable facial features like 'Nose - bridge short/long' and 'Mouth - wide/thin,' indicating a facial morphing software interface. Prof. Freeman explains, "People that have a wide sellin width, which is the nose bridge on the face, and it's a cue that really has nothing to do with anything interesting... it's just simply the how wide the bridge of the nose is." The screen then shows 'Person #9' with the text 'Helped save a lost kitten,' followed by a grid of 18 diverse digital faces. Prof. Freeman continues, "So it's an arbitrary facial feature, and 80% of the time we're pairing this wide sellin with trustworthy behaviors. So now they see completely new faces... and that have wide and narrow sellins, that arbitrary facial feature." He concludes, "And indeed what we found was that on a variety of different measures, more conscious, less conscious..." All this unfolds against a backdrop of subtle, continuous electronic music, maintaining a professional and research-oriented atmosphere focused on digital avatars and human perception.

event_blocks.summary: Prof. Jon Freeman, a man with short dark hair in a dark blue button-up shirt, speaks expressively about the automatic activation of stereotypes without conscious awareness, concluding with a reference to "a couple minutes of learning." As light, rhythmic, and slightly melancholic music begins, the scene shifts to a close-up of a digital face on a screen, featuring a young man with short dark hair and a light gray shirt. The camera then moves to a dimly lit lab where a man in a dark blue button-up shirt, illuminated by two monitors displaying the digital face and detailed MRI brain scans, navigates data with a mouse. A female narrator explains the brain's flexibility in stereotyping and the ability to learn to evaluate faces differently over the short term, noting that "John is now looking into a training method that works long-term" and that "The same principle applies to voices." The video transitions to an outdoor setting with a man with long wavy hair in a black polo shirt speaking directly to the camera against a sunlit cityscape, while a male narrator states, "Ultimately, the more we know about these mechanisms, the less susceptible we are to being deceived by first impressions." The final segment shows a woman with shoulder-length blonde hair in a gray cardigan and a man with graying hair in a dark shirt with small white dots engaged in an indoor discussion with expressive reactions, including laughter and thoughtful listening. The female narrator concludes by describing "First impressions" as "fascinating, sometimes deceptive, but always a special, even magical moment," and potentially "the start of a new relationship where we discover so much more than what we saw at first sight," as the melancholic music continues to play.

event_blocks.summary: The video concludes with a closing credit sequence, characterized by a warm, reflective tone and a gentle, melancholic piano melody with subtle string pads. Initially, a close-up features a smiling man with graying hair in a dark patterned shirt as production roles and acknowledgments appear. This is followed by a close-up of a smooth, beige robotic head with human-like blue eyes and a wide, open-mouthed expression, its blonde hair contrasting with its mechanical features. As the soft music plays, credits intercut close-ups of a man with short dark hair and a beard in a blue shirt, a man with wavy hair in a dark shirt, and a man with a beard and mustache, all displaying neutral or gentle smiles. On-screen text details credits for the film by Dorothee Kaden, camera operators, sound team, video editors, graphics, imaging, sound mixer, unit manager, production manager, and commissioning editors from HR and ARTE. The robotic head reappears, sometimes wearing a 'Furhat Robotics' hat and displaying a wide, toothy grin. Credits also acknowledge Prof. Hans-Jochen Menzel and students at Berlin’s Ernst Busch Academy of Dramatic Arts, noting production by HR in collaboration with ARTE, with the English version by Deutsche Welle 2022. The piano melody persists, maintaining the reflective mood as the sequence ends with a bald man in a light gray shirt smiling broadly, with full production credits overlaid. Consistent warm lighting highlights facial expressions and text overlays.
